data.title: Intel Foundry Services Is Materializing As The Turnaround Of The Decade
data.description:
Intel Corporation (INTC) is positioned for a major turnaround, as Intel Foundry Services [IFS] gains momentum in sourcing third-party chip designers following the announced partnership with Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), to potentially manufacture its advanced AI chips that will power autonomous driving, its Optimus humanoid robot, and SpaceX (SPACE) satellite constellation. With optimism growing for the once financially strapped chip foundry, Intel’s positioning in the market may be returning to a strong footing in the coming years, potentially turning to profitability by eFY28. Given the optimistic outlook for Intel, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating with a price target of $93/share at 16.12x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

Intel Corporation Operational Update

Intel has been circulating in the headlines after a blockbuster announcement that the once financially strapped technology giant may be in a position for a major turnaround. In early April 2026, Intel announced a partnership with Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), to bring CEO Elon Musk’s vision to light and develop an advanced chip fabrication facility to design, fabricate, and package purpose-built logic and memory chips to power SpaceX and Tesla technologies. Musk first announced plans for the Terafab in early 2026, around the time of the Tesla Q4 ’25 earnings release, hinting that the automaker would bring in-house an advanced semiconductor fabrication facility to manufacture a large proportion of the chips needed to power these autonomous engines.

Fast forward to April 2026; Musk announced a partnership with Intel to leverage its chip fabrication expertise to potentially manage the entire process net of design. This can be a major paradigm shift for Intel, which has been digesting years of overinvestment in capital equipment while clinging to the hope of attracting external semiconductor designers for foundry services. From a thematic perspective, this can be Intel’s path into further fabrication of custom silicon under multi-year partnership agreements, providing a runway to tape out multiple nodes and multiple generations of custom silicon for the next generation of compute. From Tesla’s perspective, Intel can provide its expertise in advanced packaging to streamline production for purpose-built chips for its robotaxi program, Optimus humanoid robots, and radiation-protected chips for satellite systems.

A partnership with Intel may also help Tesla accelerate chip production, where the initial plan was to ramp up to 100,000 wafers per month before scaling to 1 mm/month at full capacity. In terms of the development cadence, Tesla will likely begin volume production of the AI5 chip in mid-2027 as it develops the next generation, AI6, which may be ready for production in late 2027 and deployment in 2028. Given the timing of chip development, this could potentially align with Intel’s fabrication business, which may require capital equipment sourcing and other preparations to run at mass production. Details relating to process technology have not been disclosed, but if timing aligns and is suitable for Tesla’s chips, the chips could be manufactured either on 18A or 14A. Intel is planning to be in risk production of Intel 14A in 2028 and ramp to volume production in 2029, potentially aligning with Tesla’s development cadence.

In addition to its partnership with Tesla, Intel is currently in talks with Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL) to provide advanced chip packaging services, each of which could be major deals for Intel Foundry Services. Intel will be leveraging its latest iteration of Embedded Multi-die Interconnect Bridge-TSV [EMIB-T] technology for advanced AI and HPC chip nodes, which is expected to improve semiconductor designs’ performance, power efficiency, and flexibility, supporting HBM4 memory stacks, all developed using the Synopsys, Inc. (SNPS) 3DIC Compiler. Accordingly, Intel is preparing for scaled production, which may align with management’s comments of ramping up third-party chip production while speaking at the Second Annual AI Summit.

Deals with Amazon and Alphabet may be major revenue drivers, as each of the companies is set to deliver large quantities of training and inferencing chips to Anthropic over the coming years. Alphabet recently announced an agreement with Anthropic to deliver 3.5 GW of compute capacity built on Google TPUs. Amazon, on the other hand, recently announced committing 2 GW of Trainium compute capacity for OpenAI.

Broadening its capabilities, Intel recently announced a definitive agreement to repurchase the 49% equity interest in Fab 34 in Ireland from Apollo for $14.2b. Thematically, this can be an appealing acquisition given the growing importance of CPUs for AI inferencing as enterprises turn towards leveraging AI applications and agentic AI.

Intel Corporation Financial Forecast

As a result of the new optimism towards sourcing external customers for IFS, I am increasing my revenue and profitability forecast for the segment, expecting IFS’s profitability to turn positive in eFY28. Despite the optimistic outlook for IFS, I do not expect a significant uptick in growth until late eFY26-27 for its existing third-party customer, eFY27-28 for the hyperscalers, and eFY28 for Tesla and the hyperscalers.

For eQ1’26, I’m forecasting Intel to report $12.5b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $0.04/share, maintaining my previous forecast for the quarter. The big shift in sentiment is more geared towards its long-term growth potential for IFS, which may turn around from being a cash incinerator to a cash-generating machine.

Risks Related to Intel Corporation

Bull Case

Intel is gaining momentum in sourcing third-party IFS customers, a major transition from the challenges previously faced after years of heavy investments in capital equipment. With the shift in tone towards disciplined capital spending, Intel may also be turning towards positive free cash flow in the coming fiscal years; I’m forecasting the transition to occur in eFY27.

Bear Case

Intel is still in the early stages of talks with Amazon and Google, and agreements may not materialize. While Tesla may be a major partner to deliver substantial growth for IFS, Tesla has a history of pushing back timelines, which may impact Intel’s IFS business.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

INTC shares are trading at a 1-year high at 21.61x EV/aEBITDA, a relative premium when compared to the shares’ historical trading range. Despite the high trading premium, I believe the renewed optimism for external IFS services may deliver stronger growth over the coming years, potentially resulting in a significant mispricing of shares.

Fundamentally, I believe INTC shares should be priced at $93/share at 16.12x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA; I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating.

Using the model, the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: Oil Just Doesn't Want To Correct With Persistent Ceasefire Uncertainty - WTI Technical Analysis
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Traders were just celebrating the latest 2-week US–Iran ceasefire news, but it now seems that markets got ahead of themselves.

The initial reaction was one of a classic risk unwind – WTI crude oil gapped sharply lower from around $118 to $92 as risk premium evaporated almost instantly on the announcement. For a brief moment, it looked like the geopolitical overhang that had fueled oil’s explosive rally was finally unwinding.

But that relief didn’t last.

Since the gap lower, prices have been grinding steadily higher again, now hovering back near the key $100 level — a sign that traders are reassessing just how fragile this truce really is.

Headlines over the past 24 hours have painted a highly uncertain picture:

Iranian officials have stressed that any meaningful peace agreement must extend beyond bilateral talks, explicitly mentioning broader regional considerations, including Lebanon, and warning that the coming hours are “critical.”

At the same time, conflicting narratives between US and Iranian sources have only confirmed that the two sides are still far from trusting each other. What is sure is that traffic in the Strait of Hormuz remains more than muted, if inexistent.

A recent Reuters report indicated 6 crossings in the last 24 hours compared to the usual 140 in the same timespan – that's not the type of progress that markets want to see.

This kind of environment is particularly difficult for commodity pricing – oil thrives on clarity of supply expectations, but current conditions offer anything but that. Instead, participants are stuck in a feedback loop of headline-driven volatility, where each new comment shifts sentiment without establishing a durable trend.

The result is a market caught in limbo — no longer pricing worst-case escalation, but far from confident in a sustained de-escalation.

As WTI breaks the $100 threshold yet again, the next directional move will likely depend less on technicals and more on whether diplomacy can turn from words into verifiable action. Until then, expect volatility to remain elevated and conviction to stay thin.

As the situation looks to be more clear in the coming days, let's dive into a multi-timeframe analysis of WTI (US) Oil to determine levels of interest and put the odds in the trader's favor to capitalize on the issue.

US Oil Multi-Timeframe Analysis

WTI 4H Chart and Technical Levels

WTI has seesawed after yesterday's large drop to $92.70, having already rallied by 11% since.

After touching the oversold RSI border and the bottom of the Pivotal War support (~$93), bulls have raged back into the commodity as uncertainty still looms – A very important test is now coming closer with the 4H 50-period MA ($104.62)

  • Breaching back above would put the bullish panic thesis fully back into action
  • Rejecting it, however, would allow a more rangebound action ahead ($98 to $104 until markets get more clarity)
  • Breaking $98 again hints at a lower range ($92.70 to $98)

WTI Technical Levels:

Resistance Levels

  • 4H 50-period MA / 1H 200-MA (~$104.20)
  • $106 to $108 June 2022 Resistance
  • $110 psychological level
  • 2022 and War highs $117 to $120

Support Levels

  • $98 to $100 Major Pivot
  • War Support Pivotal $93.00 to $95
  • $87 to $90 mini-Support (4H 200-period MA)
  • 2025 Highs Key Support $78 to $80
  • Pre-War Support $63.80 to $64

1H Chart and action levels

Looking closer, WTI is once again stuck in uncertain back and forth action.

The 200-hour MA ($104.18) will be acting as a major barometer for traders.

On the immediate action, the rebound in WTI is stalling as the 1H RSI is back to overbought.

  • Traders will surely await for further news before moving on to the next trades
  • Keep an eye on a potential rangebound formation from $98 to $104 as markets await for further clarity.

Keep a close eye on the latest headlines!

Original Post

data.title: Genmab: Multiple Binaries Could Alter The Thesis
data.description:
It has been over a year since my last Genmab (GMAB) article, where I discussed Genmab’s pipeline, strategic partnerships, and impressive growth thanks to strong performances by DARZALEX and EPKINLY. Despite ongoing legal disputes with Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), I believed that the company’s upcoming Q3 earnings report was going to be a catalyst that could potentially reverse the 20% decline in the share price. Since then, the company has averaged double-digit revenue growth, and the ticker is up over 20%. However, the market’s sentiment still appears to be fixated on the company’s DARZALEX revenue, and the royalties are expected to come to an end in 2029 in the U.S. and the early 2030s in Europe. This is a major concern because DARZALEX has been the company’s primary revenue source for years, and we don’t know if the company has enough in the portfolio to offset those losses, much less continue to grow deep into the second half of the next decade. Well, I read through a recent healthcare conference presentation transcript, and it looks as if EPKINLY, Rina-S, and petosemtamab are all ready for registrational readouts that could establish Genmab’s multi-billion-dollar oncology platform and help reset the bull thesis away from DARZALEX.

Currently, GMAB has dropped under my Buy Threshold of $28.25, but a strong bounce off an anchored VWAP has me considering a buy in the near term just in case the market realizes the potential of Genmab’s pending catalysts.

I intend to discuss Genmab’s upcoming catalysts and how a clean run of positive data could transform GMAB’s bull thesis. Then, I will discuss how the Street expects Genmab to perform in the next decade. In addition, I will point out some of my leading downside risks that could come from these binary events. Finally, I take a look at the GMAB Daily Chart to see if I can identify an opportunity to add to my position while it is trading below my Buy Threshold.

Stacking Up Catalysts

As we move deeper into 2026, Genmab's outlook is centered on a pile of late-stage clinical catalysts that could define the company for the next decade. It looks as if Genmab has up to six registrational readouts slated over three lead assets (EPKINLY, Rina-S, and petosemtamab). Not only is this a substantial volume of data in a condensed timeframe, but we also have to acknowledge that these readouts will likely determine if these candidates will get through the FDA in a tight cadence. So, not only could these data readouts determine the probability of FDA approval, but we could see Genmab launching new products and indications leading to multiple value inflections.

EPKINLY’s Phase III readout in relapsed/refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) was released back in January, expected in early 2026, with EPKINLY showing a statistically significant improvement in PFS along with higher response rates and durability. However, it did not demonstrate an overall survival (OS) benefit (HR ~0.96). Although the OS miss is material, it might not prevent EPKINLY from being approved. Still, it might force a narrow label, slower adoption, and maybe require confirmatory data for full approval.

The EPKINLY DLBCL data is expected to be followed by additional combination and earlier-line studies through the first half of the year. A separate Phase III study in first-line DLBCL is also expected to report this year, which would represent a larger commercial opportunity given the larger patient population. Rina-S is expected to generate registrational Phase II data in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer in the second half of this year. Petosemtamab is expected to post Phase III data in head and neck squamous cell carcinoma at some point in the second half of the year.

The commercial opportunity of these candidates is substantial. Management outlined that EPKINLY's peak sales have the potential to exceed $3B if they can get the label expanded into earlier lines and global penetration continues in B-cell malignancies. Rina-S is projected to bring in $2B+ in peak sales from gynecologic oncology, with potential upside as it expands into extra indications. Petosemtamab is seen as a multi-billion-dollar solid tumor asset, particularly if its early efficacy marks still hold up in larger studies. Indeed, some biotech companies can post rosy internal peak sales estimates, but I wouldn’t reject Genmab’s numbers because they have already demonstrated strong clinical and commercial prowess. In fact, Genmab’s proprietary product sales grew more than 50% year-over-year in 2025.

I will also point out that these data readouts and potential approvals over the next year are going to be critical to keeping investors enthusiastic about the long-term prospects of the company. Yes, Genmab is guiding to ~14% revenue growth in 2026, but we also have to consider that they are also expected to ramp up their OpEx to support late-stage development and prepare for the launch of these assets. So, positive data readouts can reassure the market that the pending compression from investment will be worth it for the possibility of multiple launches in 2027.

Changing the Thesis

Genmab came into 2026 with strong clinical and commercial momentum thanks to DARZALEX and EPKINLY, with 19% revenue growth and 24% operating profit expansion in 2025. Looking at the company’s earnings history below, we can see that Genmab has been able to roughly double their quarterly revenue in only a few years.

Sadly, the share price has done the opposite and has gone from trading around $40 per share in Q1 of 2022 to around $28 per share today.

Why?

Well, as I mentioned in my introduction, the DARZALEX revenue is expected to dry up between 2029 and 2032 because Johnson & Johnson has been winning the court battles. Before these court rulings, a GMAB investment looked asymmetrical with no clear ceiling for over a decade. Now, the market has an end date for the DARZALEX royalties, so the outlook got flipped… despite the company continuing to report strong growth.

However, 2026 and 2027 have the potential to change the outlook with multiple registrational readouts that could enable several high-value 2027 launches. Genmab’s thesis could change from a royalty- and milestone-driven thesis to wholly owned or partnered products where the company has a say in pricing, access, lifecycle management, and global rollout… unlike with DARZALEX. I know that might not seem like a big deal, but again, the ticker’s performance has been married to DARZALEX for years, so we are possibly looking at GMAB adding a layer of insulation from DARZALEX with every new approval and launch.

The question is… Can they offset the decline in DARZALEX royalty income?

Looking at the revenue estimates below, it appears as if some Street analysts are confident that Genmab will be able to offset the loss of DARZALEX income by 2029 and 2030.

Indeed, the estimated growth with DARZALEX is double-digit, and it goes down to single-digit growth without. However, the market has been treating GMAB as if the company is doomed, and there is limited upside on the horizon. I understand that the market has to be cautious about bidding a ticker up ahead of uncertainty, but the market has pushed the share price down to discount levels for its current and projected performance. GMAB is currently trading around 4x forward price-to-sales for 2026 and 2.5x forward price-to-sales for 2030. The industry average is typically around 4x-5x price-to-sales, so the ticker is discounted for any potential growth.

Of course, we have to assume the market is going to chastise the ticker because of the surge in OpEx and the consequential drop in EPS for this year. However, Street analysts are estimating an immediate rebound in 2027 and strong double-digit EPS growth for the remainder of the decade.

I understand these are just estimates, but they do illustrate how the company can absorb the costs of building a biotech company that can survive and possibly thrive after losing its primary breadwinner. Again, the company could have the registration data from the programs that are expected to fuel this growth this year, with the possibility of those assets being approved and on the market by the end of 2027. So, we are looking at a potentially rapid transition from a DARZALEX stock to a more diversified biotech company with an impressive list of technologies and partnerships.

Risks to Consider

Although I have confidence in Genmab’s ability to execute in the clinic and on the market, I must concede there is still a significant downside that could derail my transition thesis. First and foremost, I'm relying on multiple assets to produce registration-level data that will lead to approval. Having more than one asset heading towards the finish line is in itself very impressive, but the company needs these programs to be approved if they want the product to be on the market and gain commercial traction in time to offset the looming threat of losing their DARZALEX income. Again, I have confidence in Genmab’s ability to handle these assets in the clinic, but we have to accept that producing homerun data in oncology is incredibly difficult… and even then, the data might have to be undeniably superior to the current standard of care to get the best label or be a commercial success. So, Genmab needs the 2026 data readouts to be more than just “acceptable” if they want to offset DARZALEX and continue growth in the next decade. It is possible that only one failure could devastate the share price for a prolonged period of time, because even if the company is successful with the remaining programs, the loss of one might be enough for them to fall short.

I also have to acknowledge that the risk doesn’t disappear if the data is positive and should support approval. These programs will still have to deal with several commercial risks, such as competition, payers, adoption curves, etc. So, it is possible that GMAB’s potential resurgence could lag the progress until the evidence is indisputable that all of the programs are a commercial success, and they are tracking to fill the void left by DARZALEX. Essentially, I have to see these programs as a single, high-stakes binary event.

Despite these risks, I am maintaining my GMAB conviction rating at 4 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare “Bioreactor” portfolio.

My Plan

In my previous Genmab article, I disclosed that I would not be haphazardly clicking the buy button despite GMAB trading below my Buy Threshold of $28.25 and Buy Level 1 of $23.11. However, I was able to load up on shares back in late November of 2024 and again in May 2025, but I ceased my buy activities once the ticker went on a run in late 2025. Now, the ticker is back below my Buy Threshold and has shown a strong bounce off a long-term anchored VWAP around $25 per share and is holding above the anchored VWAP from the April 2025 low.

So, I do see some indications that GMAB might be ready to return above my Buy Threshold, and possibly go on a run if these data readouts are positive. Therefore, I’m going to be on the lookout to make a minor addition to my GMAB position in the coming days, just in case the market wants to take the share price out of my price range.

If I do make a small addition, I will then look to set equivalent sell orders around my Sell Target 1 of $42 per share so I can book profits and return my position to a "house money" condition.

On the other hand, if the company reports lackluster data, I will go hands-off on the ticker until the company can address the concerns and the market resets the trading range.

Long term, I still expect to trade GMAB for at least five more years to produce profit while developing a core position for a long-term investment.

data.title: Q4 GDP Revision And February PCE: Growth Revised Down, No Relief On Inflation
data.description:
The latest batch of data reinforces a familiar but increasingly uncomfortable macro setup: growth is softening at the margin, but inflation is showing little sign of easing. The final revision to Q4 2025 GDP confirmed that economic momentum slowed more than initially reported, largely due to temporary factors tied to the government shutdown. At the same time, more timely data on February income, spending, and prices point to a consumer that continues to drive activity despite weakening income growth, with firm demand coinciding with another step higher in inflation.

Q4 GDP

The third and final release of Q4 2025 GDP data provided an even more sour view of growth than the second estimate did. Real GDP is estimated to have grown at a SAAR of just 0.5% now, down from 0.7% in the previous release. The downward revision can be blamed on new, lower estimates of consumer spending and investment growth in Q4, while the final readings of contributions from government and trade were roughly the same. The broad story is the same from the previous release: the government shutdown caused the U.S. economy to grow at the second slowest pace since Q1 2022 (slowest was Q1 2025, caused by massive imports).

Let’s go over the two major downgrades in the data:

  • Personal consumption expenditures (PCE) grew at a SAAR pace of 1.9%, only down slightly from the previous estimate of 2.0%. This revision came from lower nondurable goods spending growth of 0.4% (down from 0.6%), offset by an upward revision in durable goods spending growth of 0.3% (up from 0.0%). The estimate of services spending growth was unchanged at 2.7%.
  • Gross private domestic investment growth was revised down sharply to 2.3% SAAR (from 3.3%), marking the largest downgrade among major components. The drag came from weaker inventory accumulation, with inventories contributing just 0.14 ppts (down from 0.28), while fixed investment contribution edged lower to 0.26 ppts (from 0.29), primarily reflecting softer residential investment.

Even though there were some changes to consumption and investment, the story of Q4 2025 is still the same. The government shutdown was the dominant driver of the slowdown, with government consumption contracting at a SAAR of -5.6% (revised up slightly from -5.8% actually) and the federal component alone subtracting -0.96 ppts from headline GDP growth. Outside of that drag, private sector activity held up relatively well, as real final sales to private domestic purchasers rose at a 1.8% SAAR pace (revised down -0.1 ppts), pointing to an economy that continued to hum along despite disruptions to incomes and spending tied to the shutdown.

This is also reflected in the alternate measure of growth, real gross domestic income (GDI), which rose at a 2.6% SAAR pace in Q4 2025, down from 3.5% in Q3. Averaging the two measures, real GDP and real GDI increased 1.5% in the fourth quarter, a notable slowdown from the 4.0% pace in the prior quarter, and further reinforcing that there was a step down in overall economic momentum but not a capitulation.

Personal Income and Outlays

The BEA also released more current data covering income, spending, and inflation in February. The headline personal income growth figure came in much lower than expected, falling -0.1% MoM when an increase of 0.3% MoM was forecasted. Data also showed that disposable personal income fell -0.1% MoM and, when adjusted for inflation, dropped -0.5% MoM. This is the largest decline in overall and disposable income since May 2025. The weakness in incomes came specifically from declines in personal income receipts on assets (-0.8% MoM) and personal current transfer receipts (-0.4% MoM), which were both only partially offset by employee compensation up 0.2% MoM.

Despite the drop in incomes, consumer spending remained firm in February. Personal consumption expenditures (PCE) rose 0.5% MoM, the strongest monthly gain since August 2025, driven primarily by a surge in goods spending. Goods consumption increased 0.9% MoM, led by durable goods (+2.0%) and a sharp rise in auto spending (+4.5%), while services spending continued to expand at a steady 0.3% pace. Under the surface, spending on core categories remained particularly strong, with PCE excluding food, energy, and housing up 0.6% MoM, suggesting that underlying consumer demand held up well even as income growth weakened.

A good portion of the nominal spending strength in February can be attributed to another firm step higher in prices, largely in line with expectations. The headline PCE price index rose 0.4% MoM, the largest increase since January 2024 and matching the pace seen in both January and February 2025, leaving the YoY rate unchanged at 2.8%. Core PCE also increased 0.4% MoM, matching January’s pace, while the annual rate edged down slightly from 3.1% to 3.0% YoY. The monthly strength was driven primarily by goods prices, which rose 0.7% MoM, including a 1.1% jump in durable goods. Nondurable categories were also firm, with clothing up 1.0% and gas and energy goods up 1.4%, while services inflation remained more contained at 0.2% MoM.

Taken together, these dynamics point to persistent inflationary pressure rather than a continued disinflation trend. Price gains are broadening across categories, supported by firm consumer demand and easier financial conditions following rate cuts in Q4 2025. There is little evidence of sustained cooling in the medium-term trajectory, especially as tariffs and input cost pressures continue to filter through. Importantly, these data are already somewhat outdated, as energy prices moved higher in March and supply chain pressures spiked due to the conflict in the Middle East, suggesting that inflation readings will remain elevated across a range of goods categories over the next few months.

Market Reaction

It is hard to tease out a reaction to specific macro releases when financial markets are so sensitive to geopolitical developments in the Middle East. Indeed, with the recent ceasefire being announced, investors are laser-focused on the disagreements between the U.S. and Iran and whether or not it will hold. The bond market is playing a similar game, but it has also been sending a clearer macro message over the last month: inflation is getting worse.

The month of March saw large rises in Treasury yields, with my charts focused on the 2-year (US2Y) and 10-year (US10Y) rates, up 41 bps and 37 bps, respectively. These gains were not only driven by oil prices skyrocketing and shipping lanes becoming more complicated by the Middle East conflict, but also by the Federal Reserve sending a hawkish message on rates in the March FOMC meeting. Indeed, the March FOMC minutes released yesterday suggested that rate cuts could be needed if inflation starts to spike.

“Many participants pointed to the risk of inflation remaining elevated for longer than expected amid a persistent increase in oil prices, which could call for rate increases to help bring inflation down to the Committee's 2 percent objective and keep longer-term inflation expectations firmly anchored.”

Today’s data points broadly support this upside risk. Yes, Q4 2025 GDP did get revised down, but there wasn’t too much to be taken from that update since the story remained the same (government shutdown impeded growth). Instead, the February income and outlays data drove the macro narrative today as it revealed that consumer remains willing to spend even if income growth is negative and prices consistently increase. All together, these macro forces support higher rates and a patient Fed over the next few months.

data.title: Elemental Royalty Corp: The Tether-Backed Gold Royalty Growth Story
data.description:
Introduction

Elemental Royalty Corporation (ELE) went through a major transformation recently, becoming a mid-tier streaming company that stands to benefit from the current environment.

I rate ELE a Buy, being one of the most attractive companies in their industry and trading close to a conservative fair value while also benefitting from Tether’s involvement.

Transformative Deal Thanks To Tether

Following the merger between Elemental Altus Royalties and EMX Royalty from late 2025, funded by Tether - the same company behind USDT, which owns most of my Adecoagro (AGRO) position and has made several other investments in commodities - the company is now in an even better position for deals.

In fact, we could even argue that Tether itself is the reason why they’ve pushed for this merger in the first place, as they first invested in this back when it was Elemental Altus Royalties in June 2025, expanding their push into gold and hard asset-backed financial infrastructure.

They now have access to capital from Tether, which they call a “supportive major shareholder” - as has been the case recently for Adecoagro, getting help from them in funding their massive, transformative deal to buy Profertil that would’ve otherwise likely been out of their reach, which can be a major advantage in the future alongside better power of negotiation as a bigger, more complete, and financially stronger mid-tier royalty company, while the recent Nasdaq listing helped them boost liquidity fivefold.

In fact, throughout 2025, the company bought $68.7 million worth of royalty interests, with their $100 million private placement thanks to Tether helping them fund it alongside a $28 million debt repayment.

For 2026, the company's guidance aims at $76.5 to $94.5 million in revenue and GEOs (gold equivalent ounces) of 17,000 to 21,000, although they're assuming relatively high gold and copper prices at $4,500/oz and $5.50/lb.

Following this deal and the major jump in commodity prices, the company’s numbers jumped in the double- or even triple-digit percentage points, with the Adjusted OCF reaching ~$33.94 million on $49.2 million in revenue and 14,285 GEOs sold. If we do some simple math, based on the midpoint of their GEO guidance (19,000 oz) and assuming the same commodity prices and costs as last year, we should see a roughly 33% increase in Adj. OCF, getting them to roughly ~$45.14 million in 2026.

For the longer term, ELE now expects their strong project pipeline (especially first production/expansions at Timok, Laverton, Dugbe, Karlawinda, Diablillos, Viscaria, and Mactung) to more than double their GEO compared to 2025, which in itself is a major increase compared to prior years as the company has been developing. By 2029, they estimate GEO could reach roughly 30,000, which would be a major achievement, for a very strong CAGR of 16.44% from 2026 to 2029 - without assuming any new deals.

Financially, based on ELE’s latest report, we see a strong position, with their current assets almost covering their total liabilities and a solid cash position (before subsequent deals), being in a strong position to take advantage of more deals, especially with Tether’s backing.

They also announced their first dividend of $0.12/share (annual amount, paid quarterly), with the caveat that this is the first public gold firm to pay dividends in tokenized gold, giving shareholders the option to receive Tether Gold (XAUT-USD), a blockchain-based stablecoin pegged to physical gold.

Iran Pressure May Not Last

Something mentioned before is that the Iran conflict plays a major role in gold’s recent performance. Although some may be inclined to think that the market would buy gold hand over fist due to the significant inflation and risk of a recession it can cause, the opposite happened. At least initially, money moved into the dollar as a safe haven, strengthening the currency against others and assets such as stocks, bonds, and gold, while also boosting treasury yields.

For the foreseeable future, however, especially since this conflict can have significant effects on the global economy for much longer than the military phase, since the region’s energy infrastructure is getting more and more damaged with every day that passes, potentially leading to inflationary shocks that can last for a while, leading to uncertainty regarding the Fed’s next moves, while the ECB already presented its willingness to even hike rates as soon as possible, lifting its borrowing costs to 15-year highs.

Meanwhile, since a majority of gold’s recent demand surge came from investing demand while banks’ and jewelry demand fell, it’s fair to expect volatility, especially in such times, with these trends potentially reverting as retail/speculative demand would fade in fear when seeing such drops, while the market has to digest news and headlines such as the IEA chief saying that the Hormuz crisis is “worse than 1973, 1979, and 2002 combined” not long ago, although that was before the current ceasefire.

Sure, that’s not to say that we can’t wake up to headlines such as “Trump accuses Iran of breaking ceasefire deal …” which can rapidly turn the market into panic again. In fact, the market already got scared by something similar a day after the agreement was announced, and we can see rising skepticism over this US-Iran truce already.

Re-Pricing Potential Still Strong

As mentioned before, I believe (and hope) that a real de-escalation is very likely as a result of the significant military, logistic, religious, etc. implications described in this recent Tourmaline Oil (TRMLF) analysis, as it would be hard to win anything in an amount of time that wouldn’t be detrimental for the global economy for the foreseeable future given Iran’s massive size as an armed natural fortress, with very rough mountains, significant sandstorms, and even winters, making it a nightmare for ground troops.

Meanwhile, after the current inflationary shock caused by this conflict passes, the market’s attention can once again turn back to things that boosted gold in the first place, such as concerning rises in the levels of national debt, a falling dollar as a result of potential rate cuts, uncertainty, consumer pressure, and even Japan’s potential US Treasury selloff.

This comes as Japan’s (which is the largest foreign owner of US treasuries) own bond yields are skyrocketing - due to their economic plans and uncertainty - to levels we haven’t seen in ages, making their local bonds more attractive to hold and potentially causing them to sell treasuries to buy them, which would by default require selling dollars, weakening the currency.

Something I said in the recent Royal Gold (RGLD) analysis is that this type of business can have yet another “sowing” period thanks to this environment. Higher gold prices can result in major incentives for miners to start new projects, which can require funding from streaming/royalty companies to some degree (alongside their better cash flows and stronger balance sheets), while lower prices can also have similar effects, especially now that we’ve seen gold and silver prices jump so high, potentially leading to miners wanting to finish projects in preparation for a recovery.

Especially since we’re talking about a smaller company, they can be incentivized to work with smaller miners for deals that would otherwise not even move the needle for larger peers, which can be an advantage in this market.

As a note, given the company’s small size and how highly valued their peers are, I wouldn’t be surprised to see a larger company try to buy them, although it’s uncertain if Tether would accept, especially if they exercise their option to buy more shares.

About that, given Tether’s plans to expand their presence into “hard asset-backed financial infrastructure,” it shouldn’t be a surprise that they’re trying to tie this to cryptocurrencies and related financial instruments, especially since ELE is now the first to pay dividends in Tether Gold, and I wouldn’t be surprised to see Tether itself be interested in acquiring what’s left of ELE if this works out.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, although we’re talking about a company that’s better from many points of view following the deal, with major projects and access to a lot of capital, this company still depends on the timeline of these projects and especially the price of the commodity, even though the overall risk is lower than a miner itself.

Meanwhile, Tether’s growing presence and ties to the company can even lead to them pursuing a takeover, which can be even more attractive for them if the stock price falls as a result of market panic on lower commodity prices.

Valuation

For ELE's DCF to Equity valuation, based on the estimates mentioned above (constant costs and gold prices, guidance midpoint, as well as only assuming their potential from the current projects), we start from an FCF equivalent of $45.14 million in 2026 and a CAGR of 16.44% from 2026 to 2029 as they reach 30,000 GEOs. For the subsequent 5 years, I’m assuming a 3% CAGR to take into account potential for new discoveries and other improvements on their rights, with a note that if we want to account for potential from new investments, they would have to be deducted similar to CAPEX (in this industry, that’s through acquisitions of royalty interests), with a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a discount rate of 8%, as well as 68.8 million diluted shares outstanding as of their latest report.

This gets us to an equity value of $1.19 billion, for an intrinsic value of $17.29 per share for ELE and CAD 23.96 for (ELE:CA). If we also take into account potential merger synergies (including lower G&A costs), we shouldn’t be far from the $19.36 they’re currently trading at, which is generally good for a royalty company, as most of them are now trading at much higher levels than their fair value under similar assumptions.

Conclusion

ELE is a Buy, being a strong business that’s able to grow significantly from this point onwards thanks especially to Tether’s backing, being one of the most attractive gold royalty companies in today’s market right now.

data.title: S&P 500: Don't Buy The Dip When Macroeconomic Conditions Are Worsening
data.description:
The first quarter of 2026 is now over, and during that brief 3-month period, a lot of things have changed in relation to the annual outlook in equities. Unfortunately, not for the better.

There are a number of signs of trouble ahead, and market participants are gradually adapting to the increased probability of a market correction. A probability that I evaluated as very high just a couple of months ago in my bearish outlook for the equity market in 2026.

Q1 performance has also shown that the extreme concentration within the S&P 500 index is gradually unwinding as the equal-weighted index has outperformed its counterpart.

The market has also largely shrugged off recent geopolitical events in the Middle East, with the S&P 500 now headed to its late-February highs and volatility, albeit still high, gradually cooling off.

Even if a more permanent cease-fire is achieved and the Strait of Hormuz is fully opened, this would not remove ongoing risks for the equity market, and it is essential for investors not to be tempted to buy the dip.

Tighter Financial Conditions

Since the beginning of 2026, credit spreads have widened significantly, and although we see a gradual improvement over the past week, this is still a potential red flag for risk-on investors.

Even yields on short-term government bonds reacted. The yield on 2-year Treasuries, for example, is no longer on a downtrend trajectory and is up to almost 4%. This is above the Effective Federal Funds Rate, which usually follows the steps of the short-dated U.S. Treasuries.

That is why, in recent months, we have witnessed a major shift in investors' expectations when it comes to future FOMC decisions. Right now, the implied probabilities are that the Effective Federal Funds Rate would stay at current levels by mid-2027. This is in stark contrast to the anticipation of a number of rate cuts just a couple of months ago.

This slight tightening of credit conditions has already caused some unease in private credit investors headed for exits with record redemptions in Q1.

So far, Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL) has been in the spotlight, but almost all large private credit funds have experienced high levels of redemptions.

Debt funds managed by firms including Blackstone (BX), BlackRock (BLK), Cliffwater, Morgan Stanley (MS), and Monroe Capital have agreed to honor ~70% of the $10.1B in redemption requests they have received, the Financial Times reported.

Source: Seeking Alpha

Private credit is subprime, and as such, it is quite troubling that we see such large waves of redemptions at a time when credit spreads are nowhere near their recent highs.

Moreover, financial conditions could still be classified as loose when based on the Chicago Fed National Financial Conditions Index (NFCI). But with higher inflationary pressures and potential deterioration of the U.S. consumer, it appears highly likely that we will witness tighter financial conditions as 2026 progresses.

Inflationary Pressures and Consumers in Trouble

With the 2-week ceasefire agreement between the U.S. and Iran, the market seems relieved that a resolution to the conflict is near. But this sigh of relief could be premature, especially when it comes to the price of oil. The WTI crude oil price is still at a record high, and the implications from these higher prices would continue to put pressure on inflation and consumer spending.

Gasoline prices have skyrocketed in recent weeks, and until they return to their levels prior to the conflict, we could anticipate a further deterioration in consumer confidence.

The sluggish consumer confidence was a major risk factor that I warned about all the way back in mid-2025, and since then things have not improved materially. In February and March of this year, we saw a slight improvement in the Consumer Confidence Index, but it is still at record lows (see the graph below), and the high oil prices now threaten this weak recovery in the month of April.

Consumer confidence ticked up again in March, as a modest improvement in consumers’ views of current conditions outweighed a slight downshift in expectations for the future

Source: conference-board.org

In addition to the high oil prices, the U.S. Consumer would gradually feel more pressure through the higher mortgage rates, which are now close to 7%. Potentially higher bond yields could put even more strain on borrowing through the rest of 2026 and beyond.

And if all that is not enough to be concerned about the state of the U.S. consumer and inflationary pressures, which in turn would necessitate tighter credit conditions, the recent geopolitical event in the Middle East is also having a profound impact on fertilizer prices.

Urea prices have increased significantly in recent weeks, and although they remain below the prior highs from 2022, this now sends a strong signal that certain agricultural commodities could also follow suit in the coming months.

Investor Takeaway

Financial conditions and consumer confidence will likely deteriorate further in the coming months. If this happens, we will see more downward pressure on risk assets and especially more cyclical equities.

In the meantime, the sluggish performance of the S&P 500 in recent months has resulted in slightly lower valuations, but the cyclically adjusted price/earnings ratio remains at prohibitively high levels.

As a result of all that, my initial outlook for 2026 remains unchanged with the caveat that a systemic event within the market now appears more likely and the potential downside risk has only increased since the beginning of 2026.

data.title: RPM International: Self-Help Offers Hope, But More Is Needed To Support The Multiple
data.description:
For better or worse, the key question with RPM International Inc. (RPM) remains what it has long been – how much of a premium should investors pay for a company that offers above-average quality, but arguably not as quality as commonly thought. RPM has strong brands and an enviable track record of consistency, but metrics like tangible book value growth, ROIC, and FCF conversion don’t stand out as particularly special, particularly in light of a multiple that has expanded relative to its peers over the last two decades.

RPM shares are up a bit since my last update, thanks to a strong rally after fiscal third quarter earnings. Even with that pop, though, the shares have lagged the broader industrial space, as well as peers like Sherwin-Williams (SHW) and PPG Industries (PPG), while Sika (SXYAY) has continued to perform poorly.

I’m reluctant to overpay for RPM, but I do think the story is improving. RPM has shown it can generate better organic growth than many peers despite a difficult market environment, and I like the company’s ongoing pivot toward value-added “solutions” (vs. discrete products), as well as ongoing margin and capital efficiency efforts. I can argue for 10% to 15% near-term upside, but I’m still cautious about paying a high premium for a company that has lagged the S&P 500 (SP500) over the long term and not shown sustained improvement in key quality metrics.

A Strong Rebound In Fiscal Q3

While RPM did underperform in fiscal Q2, as I previewed in that earlier piece, the third quarter saw a nice rebound with real evidence of positive incremental margins and organic growth despite modest pricing activity.

Revenue rose 3% in organic terms, with pricing contributing about a point. This was good for a 4% beat versus Street expectations. The Consumer business saw about 2% organic contraction, a small beat, with a weak DIY market and ongoing product rationalizations weighing on results. The Construction segment generated about 7% organic growth, beating by about 5%, with an easy year-ago comp helping support mid-single-digit volume growth. The Performance segment beat by 10%, with 5% organic growth also driven by volume growth helped by easier comps.

Gross margin improved a point from the prior year (39.5% vs. 38.5%), but was weaker on a sequential basis (down over a point) in what is typically the seasonally weakest quarter.

Operating income rose 49%, beating by about 37%, with margin up about two points to 7.2%. Segment profits rose 32% (with margin up almost two points to 9.6%), with Consumer up 16% (beating by 7%), Construction up 273% (beating by 77%), and Performance up 20% (beating by 29%).

A Tricky Market Offset By Self-Help

The outlook for RPM isn’t really any clearer now than it was six months ago. Construction spending should continue to improve from here, but the war with Iran has added uncertainty to raw material costs, future interest rates, and business investment/expansion plans.

The actual impact of the conflict with Iran on RPM is nuanced. While RPM does rely on a variety of inputs that are, or are derived from, petrochemicals, about 90% of the company’s sales base is in North America and Europe, where supply concerns are low. That said, North American producers have been reluctant to significantly increase operating rates for fear of overbuilding inventories, and there is a risk that higher cost inflation will impact margins in the coming quarters and offset progress on self-help initiatives.

As far as end-markets go, construction is still more “recovering” than “recovered.” Activity should improve as 2026 moves on, and in the meantime I continue to expect RPM to benefit from higher-value opportunities in niches like data centers and pharma plants. Moreover, with new building activity more muted, there does appear to be a shift back to repair and maintenance (to extend the life and/or improve the efficiency of existing facilities) that should benefit the company.

The consumer market is still not particularly healthy. The DIY market remains weak, with a softening housing market and affordability issues both likely impacting demand.

Multiple Self-Help Opportunities

The macro situation is what it is, and there’s not much that the company can do to offset it. While RPM has historically enjoyed good pricing power, management seems to be taking a restrained approach for now, and that should help support volumes in the near term.

At the time, the company continues to invest in innovation. RPM has been focusing more on value-added turnkey solutions for commercial clients, and I expect new product flows to continue to help the company gain share, particularly in higher-value segments like data centers, manufacturing, and pharma.

The company is also accelerating its MAP 3.0 self-help program. Management has identified additional incremental manufacturing and procurement opportunities, and SG&A optimization has likewise become a bigger priority with management targeting $100M in improvements through the end of fiscal 2027. Efforts are also underway to reduce working capital intensity and allocate capital more efficiently, both of which could lead to longer-term improvements in FCF generation and ROIC.

The Outlook

While the third quarter was stronger than expected, guidance was somewhat tempered, with a wide EBIT range for Q4, the midpoint of which was moderately below Street expectations.

The biggest near-term risk I see revolves around the ongoing conflict with Iran. Higher energy and material prices are not only a threat to RPM’s margins (though pricing and procurement efforts can mitigate them to some extent), but also to the rate outlook and the economy in general, as higher energy prices are a known driver of weaker economic activity.

Longer term, the biggest risk I see here is multiple compression. RPM has a good record of consistent revenue and FCF growth, and operating/EBITDA margins have been stronger of late, but the company’s track record with FCF generation and ROIC are more mixed. I do think another three points of margin improvement are possible over the next decade, but if RPM underperforms here I do think there is a real risk that RPM’s above-average multiples will revert back toward the mean.

I’m looking for mid-single-digit growth across the next three years and over the longer term as well, with the latter growth rate around 4%. I expect the company’s margin and capital efficiency efforts to pay off with high-teens EBITDA margins and low double-digit FCF margins over time, driving around 8% FCF growth.

RPM is still pricy on discounted cash flow, suggesting even higher expectations on the Street and/or a willingness to accept an even lower discount rate as a reward for RPM’s quality. Multiples-based approaches (EV/EBITDA, P/B, et al) driven by margins, ROIC, ROE, et al are more favorable, and I can support a fair value around $120 on that basis. That still embeds a roughly 3.5x premium on EBITDA, though, so again I note the risk of multiple compression if RPM disappoints.

The Bottom Line

I’m inclined to upgrade RPM on an improved likelihood of near-term outperformance. I can argue that the shares are 10%-15% undervalued now, and I think RPM is better-placed than its peers to continue to generate organic growth despite a challenging and volatile macro backdrop, to say nothing of being well-placed for a reacceleration in the non-residential construction market in late 2026/2027.

The “but” is that I do see risk of ongoing multiple compression and I am trying to weigh the probabilities of near-term outperformance with a little more skepticism that that outperformance will persist over the long term. I’m not looking to buy RPM shares now, but I do see an argument for how the shares could outperform in 2026.

data.title: EWY: The AI Memory Supercycle Makes South Korea The Most Mispriced Market Globally
data.description:
Introduction

Previously, I’ve written a Seeking Alpha article on Micron Technology, Inc. (MU). My thesis on MU is largely based on the intense requirements of AI infrastructure creating an extraordinary level of demand for high-performance memory, which typically is a cyclical industry; however, my view is that memory companies are moving past historical cyclicality into a phase with more structural demand. The beneficiaries of this memory shortage extend beyond MU to Korean companies like SK Hynix Inc. and Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF).

I am initiating coverage on the iShares MSCI South Korea ETF (EWY) with a Strong Buy rating. My core thesis hinges on an AI memory supercycle where demand is now structural & not cyclical, an overhaul of Korean companies through the Value-Up program set to decrease the “Korea discount,” and a macroeconomic recovery that is decoupling from traditional emerging market risks.

A Concentrated Play On Memory

The traditional understanding of the memory industry is that it is bound to the cyclical fluctuations of consumer demand. However, this cyclicality is being replaced with structural demand driven by the expansion of AI infrastructure. The driver of this memory supercycle is High Bandwidth Memory (HBM). As LLMs grow in complexity, the bottleneck for AI training and inference has moved on from being raw compute power to memory bandwidth.

HBM4 requires approximately three times the wafer capacity of standard DRAM to produce the same number of bits. This increased die size significantly reduces the total number of chips available for the broader market, creating a structural undersupply in traditional memory used for PCs and mobile devices. As shown in the chart above, bandwidth and transfer speed per generation are increasing, showing that memory requirements for newer GPU generations are increasing & consequently there is more demand for memory.

According to Goldman Sachs, CapEx estimates for AI hyperscalers have risen to $527 billion in 2026 following guidance from Q3 earnings. Much of this CapEx is expected to go towards GPUs, and 66% of the cost of GPUs is represented by memory.

For investors seeking to capitalize on this supercycle, EWY provides concentrated exposure to two of the three biggest memory manufacturers: SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics. While many market-cap-weighted S&P 500 funds are often criticized for heavily weighting the Magnificent 7, the cumulative 43.5% weighting of these two memory companies within EWY is a feature and not a detracting factor in my opinion. This heavy concentration is what has allowed EWY to capture the outsized gains seen within memory manufacturers.

The high pricing increases seen in NAND/DRAM have led to a significant positive impact on EWY’s NAV. Furthermore, these price increases are not expected to slow down any time soon. TrendForce projects DRAM contract prices to increase 58% to 63% quarter-over-quarter in Q2 2026. This is essentially a further boost to the top line of memory manufacturers like Samsung and SK Hynix, and it is important to note that these pricing increases are also expanding margins, partially offsetting any additional CapEx going towards expanding capacity.

Dismantling The Korea Discount

The Korea Composite Stock Price Index trades at a forward P/E ratio of 8.8x, which seems incredibly low when compared to the S&P 500, which trades at a much higher forward P/E ratio of 21.2x. That is a 140% gap between the P/E ratios of the two indexes. Furthermore, Macquarie projects a 48% EPS growth in 2026, while the S&P 500 2026 EPS growth projection is just 14%. I believe that EWY’s holdings are going to be subject to both multiple expansion and earnings growth.

For many years, South Korean companies traded at lower valuations than their global peers due to poor corporate governance, low shareholder returns, and generally opaque family-controlled management structures. SK Hynix, for example, trades at a 4.92x forward P/E ratio, whereas MU trades at a 6.3x forward P/E ratio. I believe that this discount is being narrowed by a series of reforms known as the "Value-Up" program.

In July 2025, the government amended the Commercial Act to introduce a director's fiduciary duty to all shareholders. The goal of this amendment to the Commercial Act is to protect shareholders from actions by controlling families, such as unfair mergers or the hoarding of cash. Furthermore, the Korean National Assembly approved dividend tax reforms, reducing the tax rate on dividend income to a range of 14% to 30%.

The average total payout ratio of the Korean banking sector is expected to increase to more than 50% in 2026 from 36% in 2023. This shows that Korean companies are engaging in more shareholder-friendly behavior by optimizing their capital allocation to holding only the capital they need & returning excess capital to shareholders instead of letting it sit idle.

The South Korean Economy

The South Korean economy is skewed toward high-tech exports - in 2025, total exports from South Korea exceeded $700 billion for the first time. The KOSPI index is historically closely correlated with export performance, often leading export trends by approximately two months. The current strength in semiconductor ASPs and volume shipments suggests an excellent earnings outlook for 2026.

The "K-shaped" recovery mentioned by the Bank of Korea (BoK) highlights the widening disparity between the strong IT sector and the struggling non-IT sectors, such as traditional construction. For EWY, this is a favorable dynamic, as the fund is heavily weighted toward the outperformers.

From a monetary & fiscal policy perspective, the Bank of Korea has adopted a neutral viewpoint with the policy rate at 2.5%. Inflation slightly deviates from the 2.0% target, with the previously expected rate set to be at around 2.2%. BoK has indicated that it will focus on economic growth, not rate hikes.

EWY Construction

EWY tracks the MSCI Korea 25/50 Index. No single company can exceed 25% of the index weight. Additionally, the sum of all group entities with a weight greater than 5% is limited to 50% of the index. The index is rebalanced on a quarterly basis.

Above are the top 10 holdings in EWY. As previously mentioned, collectively Samsung and SK Hynix represent a significant portion of EWY at a 43.5% weightage. Beyond semiconductors, EWY also has significant automotive presence from Hyundai Motor and KIA Corporation. While automotive companies generally haven’t provided outsized returns, Hyundai, in the past year alone, has appreciated over 180%. Much of this share price appreciation can be attributed to a physical AI/robotics and autonomous driving technologies story. KB Financial & Shinhan, for example, are re-rating due to the Value-Up initiative & its effects on the banking sector as previously discussed.

Above are some basic metrics on EWY. EWY’s expense ratio of 0.59% is quite high, but given that most of the equities contained within EWY are not readily accessible in Western markets & these equities are very high quality in my opinion, I think it is reasonable to pay such an expense ratio. EWY has total net assets of $16.6 billion, and so I believe there are no significant liquidity issues with the fund.

Risks

South Korea’s high reliance on exports is vulnerable to global trade tensions. The potential escalation of US tariff measures could weigh on the outlook for automobiles and steel. Additionally, given semiconductors are viewed as a domestically critical sector in the US, there could be higher levels of competitive pressure arising due to governmental support to companies like Micron from regulations like the Chips & Science Act.

There are concerns regarding over-investment in AI. If the value generated from the adoption of AI tools ends up being disproportional to the high costs of the infrastructure buildout, it could be the case that hyperscalers cut CapEx spending. This cut in spending will lead to significant drawdowns for memory stocks, which EWY is highly concentrated in.

Conclusion

To conclude, I believe that EWY presents a very compelling investment opportunity for investors looking for exposure to high-quality memory producers. EWY presents exposure to the South Korean economy in the midst of significant corporate restructuring aimed at decreasing the Korean valuation discount to global companies. Additionally, I believe that while there are risks of over-investment into AI, potential trade tensions, and inflationary effects from high oil prices, I also think there is very high share price upside driven by the high concentration of memory producers.
